text: The surgeon performs a medial to lateral dissection using a no-touch technique along the tongue fascial plane, ensuring the retroperitoneal fascia remains intact while identifying the right ureter and gonadal vessels.

The surgeon identifies and ligates the ileocolic vessels at the mesenteric root. The third portion of the duodenum and the head of the pancreas are visualized and carefully separated from the mesentery.
The surgeon delicately dissects the truncal alley while ensuring the anterior superior pancreaticoduodenal vein is secured to prevent bleeding during the mesocolon traction maneuver.
The surgeon identifies and ligates the right colic muscles. After opening the right gastrocolic ligament, the D-plane is visibly recovered.
The surgeon exposes the retroperitoneal plane caudally and dissects the last jejunal loop. The right paracolic gutter is liberated, and the transverse colon is carefully dissected.

The surgeon extracts the specimen from the phanacin incision. The helioconic stamp is pulled upwards using a needle, ensuring the posterior wall of the anastomosis is secure. The stapler is then applied, and the anastomosis is checked internally for integrity.
The surgeon closes the atomy using two planes of continuous suture, ensuring the closure is secure and uniform.
The surgeon knots the suture of the second plane to the tail of the first plane, completing the closure of the anatomical structure.
